UNION WILL INVESTIGATE


THE GOMPERS CAMPAIGN

the cereal mills of the Northwest.


The textile workers, the striking
papermakers, the cereal mill en-
ployes and others are complaining
that the law of supply and demand
has been stood on its head by the
combines-that when nature is
most bounteous and the supply OF
superintendent, wages are reduced
and the cost of living advanced
same as when there is famine.


This announced advance in the
price of -four for this winter is CIV
ing the iourneymen bakers some
concern, as they fear that they Will
also be called upon to contribute
toward the celebration of harvest


home by accepting reduction or
wages. Therefore, the bakers are
considering plans to be proposed
at their next convention to form an
alliance with the organized farm
ers on a cooperative basis for the


purpose of smashing the middle
men combines.


In Chicago there has been start
ed a movement that is already


meeting with favor in many sec
lions fthe country. 1t is proposed
to urge the federal government to


establish a national bureau OF the
unemployed to be connected with
the department of the interior. The


bureau is to have offices in all the


principal cities to secure statistics


of the unemployed, furnish labor to
employers, establish workshops,
farms and build highways and EN-


ploy labor direct, and to furnish


relief to those who are involuntari-
ly idle. The bureau is to be financed
by taxing swollen fortunes at the
